content:
Stop paying for idle EC2 instances and heavy Docker containers. Here is how edge-native Wasm changes backend economics.

3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cloud computing system architecture makes you pay for the waiting time. For instance, if you create a traditional Java Spring Boot or Node.js backend deployed to AWS EC2 instance or ECS container, you have to pay for the RAM and CPU 24/7 — even if there is zero traffic.
Attempts to solve that problem gave rise to serverless functions such as AWS Lambda, which in turn caused the “cold start” phenomenon — a few annoying seconds that the container takes to wake up.
The latest technology introduced by W3C as a standard is called WebAssembly (Wasm) on the Edge. Deploying microservices developed in Rust and compiled into Wasm makes cold starts disappear and reduces the cost drastically.
1. The WebAssembly Standard
According to the W3C specification, WebAssembly is a binary language for a stack machine virtual machine. Even though WebAssembly was originally meant for deployment in the browser, implementations on the edge network like Cloudflare Workers and Fastly Compute, as well as WebAssembly runtimes like…
